create.name: CeO2
create.synthesis_para: To obtain Ce1−xCoxO2−y, Ce1−xNixO2−y and Ce1−(x+y)CoxNiyO2−z typical solid solutions, 0.099 g (0.27 mmol) CeCl3·7H2O, 0.04 g (0.67 mmol) urea and 0.4 g polyvinylpyrrolidone were dissolved in 19 mL deionized water in a Teflon bottle (inner volume: 25 mL). During the vigorous magnetic stirring process, quantitative solutions (200 μL to 1000 μL) of CoCl2 (0.05 M), NiCl2 (0.05 M) and 100 μL H2O2 (30%) were added into the Teflon bottle. After stirring for 20 minutes, the Teflon container was kept in a stainless steel autoclave. Sealed tightly enough, the autoclave was placed in a temperature-controlled electric oven and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at temperature of 180 °C for 6 h. Then, brown or yellow precipitates were centrifugally separated, with the supernatant discarded. After that, the precipitates were washed with deionized water for three times and once with ethanol, and subsequently dried in air at 50 °C for 3 h. Finally, Ce1−xCoxO2−y (dark brown), Ce1−xNixO2−y (light yellow) and Ce1−(x+y)CoxNiyO2−z (brown) powders were obtained.

create.name: CM
create.synthesis_para: A simple and facile co-precipitation method was used to synthesize the optimized Ce0.7Mn0.3O2-δ, and Ce0.7Mn0.15Fe0.15O2-δ (mole ratio based on oxides) catalysts with the nanostructure. Therefore, in both the doped and codoped ceria samples, the total mole ratio of dopant oxides is equal to 30%. For synthesis, Ce(NO3)3.6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade), Mn(NO3)2.4H2O (Merck, AR grade), and Fe(NO3)3.9H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) were used as precursors of the respective metals. All the precursor materials are analytical grade reagents and used without further purification. In all experiments, double-distilled water is used. To synthesize nanosized Mn/Fe co-doped CeO2 catalyst, the desired quantity of Ce(NO3)3.6H2O was dissolved in distilled water and stirred for 1 h on a magnetic stirrer. An appropriate amount of Mn and Fe nitrate precursors were dissolved separately in distilled water and then added simultaneously to the above solution. Then, the mixture solution was stirred vigorously for 2 h to make a clear solution. Under constant stirring, ammonia solution (Merck, 25 wt%) was added to the precursor solution drop wise until the pH value of the solution is reached to 8.5 ± 0.05. After coprecipitation occurred, the solution containing the precipitated product was again stirred for another 12 h and followed by aging for 24 h. After that, the precipitate was separated by vacuum filtration and then washed several times with distilled water until free from anion impurities. The obtained precipitates were then dried in an oven at 373 K for 12 h. The oven dried sample was crushed using an agate mortar and calcined at 773 K for 5 h (with the heating rate of 5 K min−1) in a muffle furnace to obtain Ce0.7Mn0.15Fe0.15O2–δ solid solution. The Mn-doped CeO2 sample was also prepared with the similar process by adding the manganese precursor into CeO2 solution, separately. The un-doped CeO2 was also obtained by the same method. The synthesis process of Mn/Fe co-doped CeO2 solid solution is shown with the help of schematic diagram in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). Hereafter, the synthesized Ce0.7Mn0.3O2-δ and Ce0.7Mn0.15Fe0.15O2-δ catalysts are denoted as CM and CMF, respectively.

create.name: CZH773
create.synthesis_para: Ce–Zr–M (M = Hf, Pr and Tb) mixed oxides were prepared by adopting a coprecipitation method using ultrahigh dilute aqueous solutions of the corresponding metal salts. The ratio of the metal oxide components was 50, 30 and 20 mol% Ce, Zr and M, respectively, thus forming solid solutions of the composition Ce0.5Zr0.3M0.2O2. The metal precursors employed in this investigation were used as received: (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6 (Loba Chemie, GR grade), Zr(NO3)4·5H2O (Fluka, AR grade), HfCl4 (Aldrich, AR grade), Pr(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) and Tb(NO3)3·5H2O (Aldrich, AR grade). In a typical procedure, the calculated quantities of the metal salts were dissolved separately in 500 mL of double distilled water under stirring condition and mixed together. After mixing of the metal salt solutions, the content was diluted up to 3000 mL using double distilled water. Upon complete mixing, excess ammonium hydroxide was added drop-wise until the precipitation was complete (pH = ∼8.5). The resulting slurry was filtered off and thoroughly washed with double distilled water until free from anion impurities. The mixed metal hydroxides thus obtained were kept overnight in a hood and subsequently oven dried at 393 K for 12 h. The final product was obtained after calcination at 773 K for 5 h in air atmosphere. Some portion of the calcined samples was once again heated at 1073 K for 5 h in air atmosphere. Hereafter the samples will be referred as CZMxxx where, C stands for Ce, Z for Zr, and M = H, P and T for Hf, Pr and Tb and xxx for 773 or 1073, respectively depending on the calcination temperature used. Unpromoted CexZr1−xO2 (CZ; 50:50 mol% based on oxides) was also prepared by coprecipitation method, annealed at various temperatures, characterized by different techniques and evaluated for catalytic activity.

reactions.notes: range of flow rates given as 50–60 N mL min−1. Assumed 55

create.synthesis_para: Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (68 %) with the concentration of salts of 0.667 M. After the dissolution of the salts, this mixture was added to distilled water, giving a concentration of 0.1 M. Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of 2.5 M KOH solution up to pH 11. Ultrasonic processing (35 kHz, 150 W) was used during all processes at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with a distilled water–ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept for 1 h in a muffle furnace

create.name: Ti05
create.synthesis_para: Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (68%) with the concentration of salts of 0.667 M. After the dissolution of the salts, this mixture was added to distilled water, giving a concentration of 0.1 M. Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of 2.5 M KOH solution up to pH 11. Ultrasonic processing (35 kHz, 150 W) was used during all processes at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with a distilled water-ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH=9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept for 1 h in a muffle furnace [25,26].

Cerium (III) nitrate, zyrconyl nitrate, gadolinium nitrate, and titanium (IV) chloride were used as metal precursors. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved into 500 ml distilled water containing of nitric acid (pH = 2) to give final concentrations of 0.04 M. Then, deposition was carried out by addition of aqueous ammonia at 30 °C under stirring to reach pH 10. Sonication (frequency 35 kHz, power 150 W, Sapphire UZV-4.0) was used during dissolution of salts in distilled water (10 min) and receiving the sediment (10 min). The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with distilled water (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept at 500 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace [7], [8].

Cerium (III) nitrate, zyrconyl nitrate, gadolinium nitrate, and titanium (IV) chloride were used as metal precursors. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved into 500 ml distilled water containing of nitric acid (pH = 2) to give final concentrations of 0.04 M. Then, deposition was carried out by addition of aqueous ammonia at 30 °C under stirring to reach pH 10. Sonication (frequency 35 kHz, power 150 W, Sapphire UZV-4.0) was used during dissolution of salts in distilled water (10 min) and receiving the sediment (10 min). The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with distilled water (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept at 500 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace [7], [8].

Cerium (III) nitrate, zyrconyl nitrate, gadolinium nitrate, and titanium (IV) chloride were used as metal precursors. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved into 500 ml distilled water containing of nitric acid (pH = 2) to give final concentrations of 0.04 M. Cerium (III) nitrate, zyrconyl nitrate, gadolinium nitrate, and titanium (IV) chloride were used as metal precursors. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved into 500 ml distilled water containing of nitric acid (pH = 2) to give final concentrations of 0.04 M. Then, deposition was carried out by addition of aqueous ammonia at 30 °C under stirring to reach pH 10. Sonication (frequency 35 kHz, power 150 W, Sapphire UZV-4.0) was used during dissolution of salts in distilled water (10 min) and receiving the sediment (10 min). The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with distilled water (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept at 500 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace [7], [8].

Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (68%) with the concentration of salts of 0.667 M. The use of concentrated HNO3 prevents the hydrolysis of salts, which contributes to the formation of solid solutions with a uniform distribution of doped components in the crystal lattice of ceria. After the dissolution of salts, this mixture was added to distilled water (resulting concentration of 0.1 M). Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of 2.5 M KOH solution up to pH 11. Ultrasonic processing (35 kHz, 150 W) was used during all processes at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with a distilled water-ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept for 1 h in a muffle furnace [[23], [24], [25]].

create.name: CeO2:Nd
create.synthesis_para: LnCl3 was prepared by dissolving lanthanide oxides in hydrochloric acid (1:1 in volume). 0.675 mmol of CeCl3·7H2O, 0.523 g of NaCl, and 1000 μL of LnCl3 (0.075 mol·L–1) solution (with 0.40 mol·L–1 of HCl) were dissolved in 6 mL of water and then mixed with 7.5 mL of NaOH solution (12.0 mol·L–1). The total volume of the suspension was adjusted to 15 mL. The suspension was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. The products were washed with water three times, dried at 70 °C, and then calcined at 300 °C for 1 h.

create.name: 1% Cu-DC
create.synthesis_para: Ce1–x(TM, RE)xO2−δ (TM = Cu, RE = La, Sm) catalysts were synthesized using the EDTA-citrate complexing method. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Kanto Chem) was dissolved in deionized water to prepare the nitrate solution. The cation precursors (La(NO3)3·6H2O (Alfa Aesar), Sm(NO3)3·6H2O (Alfa Aesar), and CuN2O6·3H2O (Aldrich)) to replace the Ce ion were dissolved in the prepared nitrate solution. Then EDTA (Junsei Chem) was dissolved in ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, Assay min. 28.0%) to prepare the EDTA-NH3·H2O buffer solution. The prepared nitrate solution and EDTA-NH3·H2O buffer solution were mixed, and citric acid was added so that the total cation molar ratio was 1:1:2 (cation/EDTA/citric acid). Then the pH of the mixed solution was titrated to ∼10 using ammonia solution. The pH-adjusted solution was evaporated on a hot plate at 80 °C to obtain a sticky solution. This sticky solution was heated to 168 °C to form a crispy solid. The solids were burned at 450 °C for 2 h to remove impurities and then calcined at 500 °C for 3 h in a box furnace. Initial samples were treated for 3 h at 700 or 900 °C in static air to obtain additional heat-treated samples.

create.name: CeZrCu0
create.synthesis_para: The CuO/Ce0.8Zr0.2O2 catalysts with different CuO content were synthesized using a surfactant-assisted method of nanoparticle assembly. At room temperature, 6 mmol of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was dissolved into 200 ml distilled water under ultrasound irradiation for 15 min, then 8 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 2 mmol of Zr(NO3)4·5H2O and calculated amount of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were added under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 0.5 h, 0.2 mol/l sodium hydroxide solution was gradually added into the above solution until the pH value of the mixed solution was 10, and then further stirred for about 12 h. The final suspended solution was aged at 90 °C for 3 h, washed with hot water, dried in the oven at 110 °C for 6 h, then milled and calcined at 400 °C for 4 h. The content of CuO was 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 mol%, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as CeZrCu0, CeZrCu5, CeZrCu10, CeZrCu15, CeZrCu20, CeZrCu25, CeZrCu30, CeZrCu40, respectively. In order to make clear the influence of the calcination temperature on the catalyst property, a series of CeZrCu25 catalysts calcined at different temperature were prepared in the similar manner.
